infrastructure.narrative: India's payment acceptance infrastructure is tilting decisively toward QR codes over traditional card terminals, with UPI QR deployment running 65x ahead of POS terminal rollouts. This 65:1 ratio has stabilised at its highest level after jumping 11% between October and November 2025. For lenders, this infrastructure shift signals a structural move away from card-based transaction flows toward UPI rails, fundamentally altering interchange economics and merchant payment cost structures.

atm-offsite-abs.observation: Offsite ATMs dropped to 73,693 in March 2026, down 137 units from February.
atm-offsite-abs.direction: 73,830 → 73,693, hitting the lowest count in this 6-month window.
atm-offsite-abs.inference: Banks are pulling back offsite ATM deployment, likely shifting capital toward digital acceptance infrastructure with higher transaction density.

atm-onsite-abs.observation: Onsite ATMs reached 135,638 in March 2026, up 634 units from February.
atm-onsite-abs.direction: 135,004 → 135,638, hitting the highest count in this 6-month window.
atm-onsite-abs.inference: Banks continue expanding branch-based ATM infrastructure while cutting offsite locations, concentrating cash access at high-traffic branches.

bharat-qr-abs.observation: Bharat QR codes fell to 5.63 million in March 2026, down 264,333 from February.
bharat-qr-abs.direction: 5.90 million → 5.63 million, hitting the lowest deployment level in this window.
bharat-qr-abs.inference: Merchants are abandoning Bharat QR in favor of UPI QR codes, which offer better integration with payment apps and faster settlement.

micro-atms-abs.observation: Micro ATMs stood at 1.39 million in March 2026, up 360 units from February.
micro-atms-abs.direction: 1.39 million → 1.39 million, holding steady near the middle of the 6-month range.
micro-atms-abs.inference: Rural banking correspondents maintain stable micro ATM deployment, providing essential cash access in underbanked areas where full ATMs aren't viable.

pos-bank-scan.observation: RBL Bank leads POS terminal deployment with 2.65 million units, followed by Axis Bank at 2.60 million and ICICI Bank at 2.15 million.
pos-bank-scan.direction: RBL Bank maintains its lead in merchant acceptance infrastructure across 64 active banks.
pos-bank-scan.inference: RBL Bank's aggressive merchant acquisition strategy gives it the largest card acceptance footprint, positioning it to capture higher interchange revenue as card spending grows.

pos-category-share-scan.observation: Private sector banks control 82.2% of POS terminals, while public sector banks hold 17.6%.
pos-category-share-scan.direction: Private banks dominate merchant payments infrastructure deployment by more than 4:1.
pos-category-share-scan.inference: Private banks' superior merchant onboarding capabilities and technology platforms are capturing the vast majority of card acceptance market share, leaving PSBs trailing in payment revenue opportunities.

pos-terminals-abs.observation: POS terminals reached 11.77 million in March 2026, up 104,484 from February.
pos-terminals-abs.direction: 11.66 million → 11.77 million, climbing to the upper range of this 6-month window.
pos-terminals-abs.inference: Banks are aggressively expanding card acceptance infrastructure to capture growing consumer spending and compete with UPI for merchant transaction volume.

upi-qr-abs.observation: UPI QR codes hit 761.2 million in March 2026, up 7.0 million from February.
upi-qr-abs.direction: 754.2 million → 761.2 million, reaching the highest deployment level in this window.
upi-qr-abs.inference: UPI QR codes are becoming the dominant digital acceptance infrastructure, offering merchants zero-cost deployment and instant settlement versus traditional POS terminals.

upi-qr-per-pos.observation: UPI QR codes outnumber POS terminals by 64.7x, holding at the strongest level in this 6-month window.
upi-qr-per-pos.direction: Ratio jumped from 58.3x in October 2025 to 64.7x in November, then stabilised around that peak through March 2026.
upi-qr-per-pos.inference: Digital payment infrastructure is deploying QR codes at 65x the pace of traditional card terminals. This shift toward QR-first acceptance creates a parallel payment rail that bypasses traditional card processing — reshaping transaction cost structures for merchants and reducing card issuer interchange revenue streams.

cards_stock.narrative: India's card market shows a clear divide: private banks dominate credit cards (71% share, 118.6M outstanding) while public banks lead debit cards (63% share, 1.045B outstanding). Both segments are growing steadily with credit cards adding 929K and debit cards 3.0M in March 2026. The concentration story is striking — top 3 credit card issuers control 67.5M cards while 8 banks issue zero, highlighting the capital and risk management barriers in credit card business versus the broad-based debit card distribution driven by account opening requirements.

cc-bank-scan.observation: HDFC Bank leads credit card issuance with 26.3M cards, followed by SBI at 22.1M and ICICI at 19.1M.
cc-bank-scan.direction: Top 3 banks control 67.5M cards while 8 banks issue zero cards, creating a 26.3M card gap between leader and laggards.
cc-bank-scan.inference: Credit card market is hyper-concentrated among large private and public banks, with smaller players locked out entirely.

cc-category-share-scan.observation: Private sector banks hold 71.0% of all credit cards outstanding versus public sector banks at 23.8%.
cc-category-share-scan.direction: Private banks command nearly 3x the market share of public banks, with foreign banks at 3.7% and small finance banks at 1.5%.
cc-category-share-scan.inference: Credit card penetration strategy favors private banks' customer base and underwriting capabilities over public sector reach.

cc-outstanding-abs.observation: Credit cards outstanding hit 118.6M in March 2026, adding 929K cards from the prior period.
cc-outstanding-abs.direction: Steady climb from 114.1M in October 2025 → 118.6M in March 2026, reaching a 6-period high.
cc-outstanding-abs.inference: Credit card growth remains resilient at 0.8% monthly, suggesting sustained consumer appetite and issuer confidence in credit quality.

dc-category-share-scan.observation: Public sector banks dominate debit cards with 62.9% share, while private banks hold 25.1%.
dc-category-share-scan.direction: Public banks lead by 37.8 percentage points over private banks, with payment banks capturing 8.3% and small finance banks 3.2%.
dc-category-share-scan.inference: Debit card distribution reflects public banks' branch network advantage and account base penetration in mass market segments.

dc-outstanding-abs.observation: Debit cards outstanding reached 1.045B in March 2026, up 3.0M cards from February.
dc-outstanding-abs.direction: Growth accelerated from 1.027B in October 2025 → 1.045B in March 2026, hitting a 6-period peak.
dc-outstanding-abs.inference: Debit card issuance maintains steady expansion driven by financial inclusion initiatives and digital payment adoption.

credit_card_txn.narrative: Credit card transactions show a clear channel migration story: POS rebounded strongly in March with ₹78.5 trillion value and 284 million transactions, but its share of total CC activity continues declining to 35.68% by value and 49.13% by volume — the lowest levels in six months. Digital channels are capturing the growth premium, while the tiny 'other' category surged to record highs of ₹90.2 billion and 3.34 million transactions, suggesting new payment innovations are finding traction. Physical retail is healthy but losing relative ground to the digital-first economy.

cc-atm-val-abs.observation: Credit card ATM cash withdrawals reached ₹4.08 billion in March 2026, the highest level in 6 months.
cc-atm-val-abs.direction: Value jumped ₹3.64 billion → ₹4.08 billion, adding ₹433 million month-on-month.
cc-atm-val-abs.inference: Cash demand rebounded after February's dip, but remains a shrinking fraction of total credit card activity as digital payments dominate.

cc-atm-val-share.observation: ATM cash withdrawals represent just 0.19% of total credit card transaction value, the lowest share on record.
cc-atm-val-share.direction: Share edged down 0.20% → 0.19%, continuing the structural decline.
cc-atm-val-share.inference: Credit cardholders are moving away from cash — ATM withdrawals are becoming negligible as a use case.

cc-atm-vol-abs.observation: Credit card ATM transaction volume hit 707,718 in March 2026, rebounding strongly from February's low.
cc-atm-vol-abs.inference: While transaction count recovered, the structural shift toward non-cash usage continues to limit ATM relevance.

cc-atm-vol-share.observation: ATM cash withdrawals account for just 0.12% of all credit card transactions, matching the historical minimum.
cc-atm-vol-share.direction: Volume share slipped 0.13% → 0.12%, hitting the floor of this data series.
cc-atm-vol-share.inference: Credit cards are now overwhelmingly used for payments, not cash access — ATM functionality is approaching irrelevance.

cc-ecom-val-abs.observation: Credit card ecommerce spending surged to ₹1.41 trillion in March 2026, the highest monthly value ever recorded.
cc-ecom-val-abs.direction: Ecommerce value jumped ₹1.10 trillion → ₹1.41 trillion, adding ₹312 billion in one month.
cc-ecom-val-abs.inference: March delivered a massive ecommerce surge — likely driven by year-end shopping and festival spending before the new fiscal year.

cc-ecom-val-share.observation: Ecommerce transactions now represent 64.09% of all credit card spending, reaching a new peak.
cc-ecom-val-share.direction: Share climbed 61.79% → 64.09%, gaining 2.30 percentage points in one month.
cc-ecom-val-share.inference: Credit cards are increasingly becoming ecommerce-first instruments, with nearly two-thirds of spending happening online.

cc-ecom-vol-abs.observation: Credit card ecommerce transaction volume reached 290 million in March 2026, setting a new monthly record.
cc-ecom-vol-abs.direction: Volume surged 248 million → 290 million transactions, adding 43 million transactions.
cc-ecom-vol-abs.inference: The ecommerce boom extends beyond value to transaction frequency — cardholders are making more online purchases than ever.

cc-ecom-vol-share.observation: Ecommerce transactions account for 50.17% of all credit card activity, marginally exceeding half for the first time.
cc-ecom-vol-share.direction: Share nudged up 50.07% → 50.17%, crossing the 50% threshold.
cc-ecom-vol-share.inference: Credit cards have reached a tipping point — more transactions now happen online than offline, marking a structural shift in consumer behavior.

cc-other-val-abs.observation: Credit card 'other' transactions hit ₹90.2 billion in March 2026, the highest level in six months.
cc-other-val-abs.direction: Value surged from ₹75.7 billion → ₹90.2 billion, adding ₹14.5 billion month-over-month.
cc-other-val-abs.inference: This microscopic channel is growing aggressively but remains negligible at 0.04% of total CC value.

cc-other-val-share.observation: Credit card 'other' transactions held steady at 0.04% of total CC transaction value.
cc-other-val-share.direction: Share unchanged at 0.04% despite absolute growth, indicating total CC pool expanded proportionally.
cc-other-val-share.inference: Even with strong absolute growth, this channel cannot gain meaningful share against the dominant ecommerce and POS flows.

cc-other-vol-abs.observation: Credit card 'other' transaction volume reached 3.34 million transactions, a six-month high.
cc-other-vol-abs.direction: Volume jumped from 2.83 million → 3.34 million transactions, up 513,000 month-over-month.
cc-other-vol-abs.inference: Transaction count is accelerating faster than value, suggesting smaller-ticket adoption in this niche channel.

cc-other-vol-share.observation: Credit card 'other' transactions captured 0.58% of total CC transaction volume, the highest share on record.
cc-other-vol-share.direction: Volume share edged up from 0.57% → 0.58%, reaching the upper range for this metric.
cc-other-vol-share.inference: Gradual volume share gains suggest this channel is finding a specific use case, though still marginal overall.

cc-pos-val-abs.observation: Credit card POS transaction value reached ₹78.5 trillion, rebounding from February's low.
cc-pos-val-abs.direction: POS value recovered from ₹67.4 trillion → ₹78.5 trillion, adding ₹11 trillion month-over-month.
cc-pos-val-abs.inference: POS remains the physical retail backbone with strong month-over-month recovery, though below October's ₹88.4 trillion peak.

cc-pos-val-share.observation: Credit card POS transactions captured 35.68% of total CC value, the lowest share in six months.
cc-pos-val-share.direction: POS share dropped from 37.96% → 35.68%, losing 2.3 percentage points despite absolute growth.
cc-pos-val-share.inference: POS is losing ground to ecommerce channels even as physical retail spending recovers, signaling a structural shift toward digital payments.

cc-pos-vol-abs.observation: Credit card POS transaction volume hit 284 million transactions, the highest count in six months.
cc-pos-vol-abs.direction: POS volume surged from 243 million → 284 million transactions, up 41 million month-over-month.
cc-pos-vol-abs.inference: Physical retail card usage is rebounding strongly by transaction count, indicating healthy in-store spending activity.

cc-pos-vol-share.observation: Credit card POS transactions held 49.13% of total CC volume, near the six-month low.
cc-pos-vol-share.direction: POS volume share slipped from 49.23% → 49.13%, continuing a gradual decline from 50.4% in November.
cc-pos-vol-share.inference: POS is losing transaction market share to digital channels despite strong absolute growth, reflecting the ongoing shift to online spending.

debit_card_txn.narrative: Debit card POS cash withdrawals have become operationally irrelevant to India's payment ecosystem. With both value and volume shares at effectively zero, cardholders have abandoned this channel entirely in favour of ATMs for cash needs and direct POS purchases for spending. The 21% volume decline since Oct 2025 to just 9,984 monthly transactions signals this infrastructure serves no meaningful role in debit card economics. Banks and merchants can deprioritise POS cash withdrawal capabilities without impacting customer experience or transaction flows.

dc-atm-val-abs.observation: ATM debit card withdrawals reached ₹2.48L Cr in March 2026, up ₹18,177 Cr from February.
dc-atm-val-abs.direction: ₹2.30L Cr → ₹2.48L Cr, returning to upper range after February dip.
dc-atm-val-abs.inference: Cash demand bounced back strongly in March, likely driven by year-end spending and festival season requirements.

dc-atm-val-share.observation: ATM cash withdrawals captured 86.75% of total debit card transaction value, down 0.67 points from February.
dc-atm-val-share.direction: 87.42% → 86.75%, continuing gradual erosion from the 87.3% peak in November.
dc-atm-val-share.inference: Despite absolute growth, ATM's dominance is slowly weakening as other payment modes gain traction.

dc-atm-vol-abs.observation: ATM withdrawal volume hit 44.4 Cr transactions in March, up 2.95 Cr transactions from February's low.
dc-atm-vol-abs.direction: 41.5 Cr → 44.4 Cr transactions, recovering to near the 6-month median.
dc-atm-vol-abs.inference: Transaction frequency normalized after February's seasonal dip, showing stable consumer cash withdrawal patterns.

dc-atm-vol-share.observation: ATM withdrawals represented 81.42% of all debit card transactions by volume, down 0.46 points from February.
dc-atm-vol-share.direction: 81.88% → 81.42%, staying in the upper range but trending lower.
dc-atm-vol-share.inference: Even as ATM usage remains dominant, the slight erosion signals gradual adoption of alternative payment channels.

dc-ecom-val-abs.observation: Debit card ecommerce transactions reached ₹13,434 Cr in March, up ₹2,324 Cr from February and hitting a 6-month high.
dc-ecom-val-abs.direction: ₹11,110 Cr → ₹13,434 Cr, marking the strongest monthly reading in this dataset.
dc-ecom-val-abs.inference: Digital payment adoption is accelerating, with ecommerce debit card usage reaching unprecedented levels.

dc-ecom-val-share.observation: Ecommerce captured 4.69% of total debit card transaction value, up 0.47 points from February to a 6-month peak.
dc-ecom-val-share.direction: 4.22% → 4.69%, breaking above the previous high of 4.5% from December.
dc-ecom-val-share.inference: Digital commerce is gaining structural share in the debit card ecosystem, reflecting changing consumer payment preferences.

dc-ecom-vol-abs.observation: Ecommerce debit card transaction volume hit 2.63 Cr in March, up 36.5 lakh transactions from February.
dc-ecom-vol-abs.direction: 2.27 Cr → 2.63 Cr transactions, reaching the upper range of the 6-month window.
dc-ecom-vol-abs.inference: Higher transaction frequency in digital channels shows consumers are making ecommerce a regular payment habit.

dc-ecom-vol-share.observation: Ecommerce transactions comprised 4.82% of total debit card volume in March, up 0.35 points from February to a new peak.
dc-ecom-vol-share.direction: 4.48% → 4.82%, surpassing the prior high and extending the upward trend.
dc-ecom-vol-share.inference: Digital payment adoption is broadening beyond just value to transaction frequency, signaling a fundamental behavioral shift.

dc-other-val-abs.observation: Debit card 'other' transactions reached ₹168 Cr in value, hitting peak levels in the 6-month window.
dc-other-val-abs.direction: ₹163 Cr → ₹168 Cr, up ₹5.1 Cr from February.
dc-other-val-abs.inference: Though a tiny segment, other transactions (likely ecommerce/online) are at their strongest value levels yet.

dc-other-val-share.observation: Other transactions held steady at 0.1% of total debit card transaction value.
dc-other-val-share.inference: Despite value growth, other transactions remain negligible in the overall debit card mix.

dc-other-vol-abs.observation: Other transaction volume rose to 78,017 transactions, up from February's 73,930.
dc-other-vol-abs.inference: Volume growth lags behind value growth, suggesting higher-value transactions in this channel.

dc-other-vol-share.observation: Other transactions held at 0.0% of total debit card transaction volume.
dc-other-vol-share.inference: Volume share remains immaterial despite absolute growth.

dc-pos-val-abs.observation: POS transaction value jumped to ₹24,346 Cr, the biggest month-on-month gain in this window.
dc-pos-val-abs.direction: ₹21,862 Cr → ₹24,346 Cr, surging ₹2,485 Cr from February.
dc-pos-val-abs.inference: Physical retail spending through debit cards rebounded sharply, signaling strong consumer activity.

dc-pos-val-share.observation: POS transactions captured 8.5% of total debit card value, up from 8.3%.
dc-pos-val-share.inference: POS is gaining share within debit transactions, showing its strength relative to ATM withdrawals.

dc-pos-vol-abs.observation: POS transaction volume reached 74.9 million, recovering from February's low of 69 million.
dc-pos-vol-abs.direction: 69.0 million → 74.9 million transactions, adding 6 million transactions.
dc-pos-vol-abs.inference: Physical retail transaction frequency rebounded alongside value, indicating broad-based usage growth.

dc-pos-vol-share.observation: POS transactions made up 13.7% of total debit card volume, up slightly from 13.6%.
dc-pos-vol-share.inference: POS continues to capture a larger slice of debit transaction frequency, reinforcing the physical retail trend.

dc-pos-withdrawal-val-abs.observation: Debit card POS cash withdrawals reached ₹17,029 thousand in Mar 2026, up from ₹16,749 thousand in Feb 2026.
dc-pos-withdrawal-val-abs.direction: ₹16,749 thousand → ₹17,029 thousand, a recovery of ₹280 thousand after declining from ₹19,954 thousand in Oct 2025.
dc-pos-withdrawal-val-abs.inference: The modest rebound suggests stabilisation at depressed levels, but values remain 15% below the Oct 2025 peak, indicating this channel has structurally contracted.

dc-pos-withdrawal-val-share.observation: POS cash withdrawals represent 0.00% of total debit card transaction value across all periods.
dc-pos-withdrawal-val-share.direction: Flat at 0.00% with no measurable change over the 6-month window.
dc-pos-withdrawal-val-share.inference: This channel is operationally irrelevant to debit card economics — essentially all debit card value flows through other channels.

dc-pos-withdrawal-vol-abs.observation: POS cash withdrawal volumes dropped to 9,984 transactions in Mar 2026, the lowest reading in 6 months.
dc-pos-withdrawal-vol-abs.direction: 10,737 transactions → 9,984 transactions, extending a steady decline from 12,577 transactions in Oct 2025.
dc-pos-withdrawal-vol-abs.inference: Usage is collapsing systematically — down 21% from peak levels as cardholders abandon this channel entirely.

dc-pos-withdrawal-vol-share.observation: POS cash withdrawals account for 0.00% of total debit card transaction volumes across all tracked periods.
dc-pos-withdrawal-vol-share.direction: Consistently at 0.00% with no variation over 6 months.
dc-pos-withdrawal-vol-share.inference: Transaction share confirms value share findings — this is a dead channel with no material impact on debit card flows.
